headline: xAI Launches Grok Build CLI in Beta: Agentic Coding Agent with Plan Mode and Parallel Subagents
summary: xAI's Grok Build CLI launched in beta on May 25 for SuperGrok and X Premium Plus subscribers. The terminal-based agent supports complex coding workflows via natural-language prompts, shows clean diffs before execution, runs up to 8 parallel subagents in headless mode, and is MCP-compatible. It runs on the Grok Build 0.1 model with a 256k-token context window at $1/$2 per million input/output tokens. Introductory pricing is $99/month for the first six months (normally $299/month).
foryou: Direct Claude Code and Codex competitor with plan-mode diffs, 8 parallel subagents, and MCP support — worth a benchmark run on your agentic pipelines this week.

headline: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus Solves 9 Open Erdős Problems and 44 OEIS Conjectures
summary: AlphaProof Nexus, a system pairing a large language model with the Lean formal proof assistant, autonomously cracked 9 out of 353 open Erdős problems (including two open for 56 years) and proved 44 out of 492 open OEIS conjectures, at inference costs of a few hundred dollars per problem. The system proposes proofs which Lean then formally verifies, eliminating hallucinated reasoning. Results were published as arXiv preprint 2605.22763v1 on May 21, 2026.
foryou: Formal proof verification as a post-hoc reasoning check is the real pattern here — watch for this LLM + verifier architecture spreading into code correctness and agentic QA pipelines.

headline: Gemini 3.5 Flash: Best at Its Speed Tier but Not Competitive with Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5 Outside Latency-Sensitive Workloads
summary: Zvi Mowshowitz's deep analysis judges Gemini 3.5 Flash as the best model at its speed tier, outscoring Gemini 3.1 Pro on Terminal-Bench and MCP Atlas benchmarks while running 4x faster. However, it falls short against Anthropic Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5 outside latency-critical applications. Google is positioning it as a daily driver for agentic workflows where speed matters more than peak capability.
foryou: Default swap-in for high-frequency agentic loops where Opus 4.7 latency is a bottleneck — already ahead of 3.1 Pro on MCP Atlas, so the pipeline upgrade is straightforward.

specs.label: Position
specs.value: Best at speed tier; behind frontier outside latency-critical tasks

headline: BenchBench: A Benchmark That Tests Whether Models Can Design Benchmarks — Only GPT-5.2 Passes
summary: BenchBench is a meta-benchmark that evaluates a model's ability to create a useful, unsolvable benchmark for other models — testing creativity and self-awareness rather than just problem-solving. In initial results, GPT-5.2 was the sole model to pass; Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.5, and others all failed to generate benchmarks that other models couldn't trivially solve. It doubles as a proxy test for benchmark-contamination detection.
foryou: If your eval pipeline relies on LLM-generated test cases, this shows most frontier models can't yet design robust evals — a real constraint for automated agentic QA loops.

headline: GPT-5.6 Leaked: Focused on Multi-Step Reasoning, Agentic Workflows, and Frontend Generation — Targeting June Release
summary: Leaked details suggest GPT-5.6 is coming in June 2026 with a heavy focus on stronger multi-step reasoning, improved agentic workflow execution, and enhanced frontend code generation capabilities. This positions it as a more agent-optimised model rather than a raw capability jump over GPT-5.5.
foryou: If the agentic workflow improvements hold, this could shift the default model choice for orchestration layers — keep an eye on API changelogs in early June.

headline: AI Hardware Market Is Becoming a Stack of Memory Problems, Not a Compute Race
summary: A deep-dive analysis argues the AI hardware market's primary constraint is shifting from raw compute to memory bandwidth and capacity. Software and model architectures iterate quickly, but hardware changes slowly — meaning chip vendors need to design architectures that remain useful as the bottleneck migrates. The piece highlights that every major workload bottleneck over the next cycle is fundamentally a memory problem.

headline: Pope Leo XIV's AI Encyclical: Environmental Costs, Algorithmic Harm, Power Concentration, and a Call for Governance
summary: Pope Leo XIV released a formal encyclical on the ethics of AI in modern society, addressing the environmental impact of training and inference, risks from opaque algorithmic systems making high-stakes decisions, and how AI amplifies the power asymmetry between resource-rich and resource-poor actors. The document is unusually readable for a papal text and explicitly calls for international governance frameworks. Simon Willison's analysis and a link to the full document are in the linked post.

headline: DeepSeek's $10 Trillion Grand Strategy: Reshape China's AI Hardware Ecosystem to Achieve a $1T Valuation
summary: A 35-minute analysis argues DeepSeek's real objective is not model sales but shaping a $10 trillion Chinese AI hardware ecosystem. Every efficiency innovation DeepSeek ships reduces dependence on top-tier Western hardware, making China's existing storage, chip, and network infrastructure sufficient for frontier workloads. DeepSeek is simultaneously raising a $10B funding round (70B yuan) with investors told the priority is AGI research over short-term commercialisation.

headline: On-Policy Distillation: Close the Train-Inference Distribution Gap with a One-Line Code Swap
summary: On-policy distillation trains a student model on trajectories sampled from its own policy while a teacher provides dense token-level KL-based supervision, closing the distribution mismatch that plagues off-policy methods. The canonical formulation unifies forward-KL, reverse-KL, and JSD losses — with reverse-KL emerging as the default for mode-seeking smaller students. On top of an RL stack like Tinker, the switch is a single line: swap the regulariser model.
foryou: If you're distilling a smaller model for a specific agentic task, switching from off-policy to on-policy distillation is a one-liner that meaningfully closes the evaluation gap — worth testing before any custom fine-tune.

headline: Models.dev: Open API for Model Specs and Pricing Across All Major Providers
summary: Models.dev consolidates specifications and pricing for models across all major AI providers into a single queryable API. It's openly accessible and designed to be piped into tooling rather than browsed manually — useful for cost dashboards, model selection logic, or automated alerting on pricing changes.
foryou: Pipe the API into your cost monitoring layer to auto-alert when a provider changes pricing mid-month — saves the manual cross-checking that breaks budget forecasts on agentic pipelines.
